description: Fix bugs discovered during a thorough audit of the CLI and plugin-tools packages. Covers missing fetch timeout, Windows compatibility for npm spawns, Linux fs.watch file recreation, and Windows tilde path resolution.

userStories.title: Add fetch timeout to notifyServer in plugin-tools build command
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want opentabs-plugin build to never hang indefinitely so that builds always complete even when the MCP server is unresponsive.
acceptanceCriteria: The fetch call in notifyServer includes signal: AbortSignal.timeout(5_000)
If the server doesn't respond within 5 seconds, the build completes normally (notification is best-effort)
userStories.notes:
File: platform/plugin-tools/src/commands/build.ts lines 202-212.

The `notifyServer` function calls `fetch()` without an `AbortSignal.timeout()`. Every other fetch call in the codebase uses timeouts:
- platform/cli/src/notify-server.ts uses AbortSignal.timeout(3_000)
- platform/cli/src/commands/status.ts uses AbortSignal.timeout(5_000)
- platform/cli/src/commands/doctor.ts uses AbortSignal.timeout(5_000)
- platform/cli/src/commands/audit.ts uses AbortSignal.timeout(5_000)

Fix: Add `signal: AbortSignal.timeout(5_000)` to the fetch options object at line 203.

userStories.title: Fix missing platformExec for npm spawns in CLI plugin command (Windows)
userStories.description: As a Windows user, I want opentabs plugin search, install name resolution, and remove to work correctly so that plugin management functions on all supported platforms.
acceptanceCriteria: packageExistsOnNpmAsync uses platformExec('npm') instead of bare 'npm'
fetchPackageInfo uses platformExec('npm') instead of bare 'npm'
npmSearchPlugins uses platformExec('npm') instead of bare 'npm'
All three functions work on Windows (npm.cmd is used via platformExec)
userStories.notes:
File: platform/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts.

Three functions use bare 'npm' string with spawn/spawnSync:
1. `packageExistsOnNpmAsync` (line 91): `spawnProcessAsync('npm', ['view', ...])`
2. `fetchPackageInfo` (line 263): `spawnProcessSync('npm', ['view', ...])`  
3. `npmSearchPlugins` (line 335): `spawnProcessSync('npm', ['search', ...])`

Other call sites in the same file correctly use `platformExec('npm')` — see lines 161, 235, 243, 501. The `platformExec` function is already imported from `@opentabs-dev/shared` at line 24.

Fix: Replace `'npm'` with `platformExec('npm')` in all three locations. The `spawnProcessSync` and `spawnProcessAsync` wrappers accept the command as the first arg, so this is a direct string replacement.

userStories.title: Fix Windows tilde path resolution in resolveStoredPluginPath
userStories.description: As a Windows user, I want tilde-prefixed plugin paths (e.g., ~\my-plugin) to resolve correctly so that plugin path comparisons work on all platforms.
acceptanceCriteria: resolveStoredPluginPath in cli/commands/config.ts handles both ~/ and ~\ prefixes
resolvePluginPathForComparison in plugin-tools/commands/build.ts handles both ~/ and ~\ prefixes
On Windows, a path stored as ~\my-plugin resolves to the correct absolute path

userStories.title: Fix logs follow mode on Linux after log file recreation
userStories.description: As a Linux user, I want opentabs logs -f to continue tailing after the MCP server restarts and creates a new log file so that I don't need to manually restart the logs command.
acceptanceCriteria: When the log file is deleted and recreated (e.g., server restart), the watcher detects this and resumes tailing the new file
On Linux (inotify), the watcher recovers from file deletion by watching the parent directory or using a polling fallback
On macOS, existing behavior is preserved (FSEvents handles file recreation natively)
Offset resets to 0 when the file is recreated
userStories.notes:
File: platform/cli/src/commands/logs.ts lines 80-151 (`followFile`).

The function uses `watch(filePath, ...)` (line 136) which watches a specific file inode. On Linux (inotify), when the file is deleted and recreated, the watcher stops firing events because the original inode no longer exists. The `readNewContent` function (line 86) already handles the case where `statSync` throws (file deleted) by returning early (line 94-96), but the watcher itself never fires again after deletion.

Fix approach: Watch the parent directory instead of the specific file. Use `watch(dirname(filePath), ...)` and filter for events on the target filename. This handles file recreation on all platforms because the directory inode persists across file deletion/recreation. When the file reappears, `readNewContent` naturally handles the truncation case (line 98-101 resets offset to 0 when `currentSize < offset`). The cleanup function (line 138-141) should close the directory watcher.

Alternative approach: Use a polling interval as a fallback. After detecting file deletion in `readNewContent`, set up a `setInterval` that checks if the file has reappeared, and if so, recreate the watcher. This is simpler but adds polling latency.

The parent-directory approach is preferred since it matches how `tail -f` works on Linux.
